summary: Patrick reviews Steph Grenier's ebook on generating website traffic, finding it too introductory for anyone already familiar with internet marketing basics. The book covers 11 topics in roughly 11 pages each, resulting in surface-level treatment that won't teach experienced ISV operators anything new. He singles out pages 52-54, a case study of using a free calculator as linkbait, as genuinely excellent and wishes the entire book matched that level of specificity. He also raises concerns about affiliate links embedded within ebook content, warning that the practice blurs the line between genuine advice and paid promotion, and could turn the microISV community into a pyramid of people selling marketing ebooks to each other.
key_points: The ebook covers too many topics at insufficient depth — 11 pages per subject yields only beginner-level introductions useless to anyone already reading ISV blogs
Case studies with real examples, like the three pages on using a free calculator to double traffic, are dramatically more valuable than abstract advice
Affiliate links embedded in ebook content create a conflict of interest that makes it impossible for readers to distinguish genuine recommendations from commission-driven ones
The microISV community risks becoming an MLM-like ecosystem where members sell marketing products to each other rather than building software that generates external revenue
Keyword density is a useless SEO metric compared to link-building strategies, yet the book prioritizes the former over the latter
money_quotes: If you have done any significant reading on the topics, this e-book will not teach you much that you don't know.
If you're running an ISV, this is almost certainly going to be akin to having a computer programmer sit through a middle school Algebra I lecture.
If the entire book was like these three pages I'd be telling everybody I knew to go out and buy it today, but sadly they are an anomaly.

Once you start down this road, it becomes difficult for the reader to differentiate between the advice that you're giving because it is solid advice and the advice you are giving because it offers a solid commission.

Instead of focusing on selling products of value to customers, we start down the merry path of cannibalizing members of our community for revenue by selling them on the dream of being a successful uISV.

Instead of being an involved community of software entrepreneurs, it would be a community of MLM hucksterism, which does not bring value to anyone and doesn't generate any revenue from outside the pyramid.
key_insight: Marketing advice grounded in specific case studies with real data is exponentially more valuable than generic introductory material, and communities should be wary of affiliate-driven content ecosystems that generate revenue from within rather than from external customers.
